repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
<!--
  Thank you for contributing to Neovim!
  If this is your first time, check out https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#pull-requests-prs
  for our PR guidelines.
-->

## Problem

`vim.pack` doesn't provide a code action for updating all plugins.

## Solution

Offer a new code action (scoped to the source file) for updating all plugins.

comment_data.body: Although this isn't documented in the protocol, it matches VS Code's behavior: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/9b7643f90393b9ad2c5d5cbbdad70fa928090009/src/vs/editor/contrib/codeAction/common/types.ts#L71
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -195,36 +197,66 @@ end
 
 --- @alias vim.pack.lsp.CodeActionContext { diagnostics: table, only: table?, triggerKind: integer? }
 
+--- @param bufnr integer
+--- @return boolean
+local function has_pending_updates(bufnr)
+  local in_update = false
+  for _, l in ipairs(vim.api.nvim_buf_get_lines(bufnr, 0, -1, false)) do
+    local group = l:match(group_header_pattern)
+    if group then
+      in_update = group == 'Update'
+    elseif in_update and l:match(plugin_header_pattern) then
+      return true
+    end
+  end
+  return false
+end
+
 --- @param params { textDocument: { uri: string }, range: vim.pack.lsp.Range, context: vim.pack.lsp.CodeActionContext }
 --- @param callback function
 methods['textDocument/codeAction'] = function(params, callback)
   local bufnr = get_confirm_bufnr(params.textDocument.uri)
-  local empty_kind = vim.lsp.protocol.CodeActionKind.Empty
-  local only = params.context.only or { empty_kind }
-  if not (bufnr and vim.tbl_contains(only, empty_kind)) then
-    return callback(nil, {})
-  end
-  local plug_data = get_plug_data_at_lnum(bufnr, params.range.start.line + 1)
-  if not plug_data.name then
+  if not bufnr then
     return callback(nil, {})
   end
+  local empty_kind = vim.lsp.protocol.CodeActionKind.Empty
+  local source_kind = vim.lsp.protocol.CodeActionKind.Source
+  local only = params.context.only
 
-  local function new_action(title, command)
-    return {
-      title = ('%s `%s`'):format(title, plug_data.name),
-      command = { title = title, command = command, arguments = { bufnr, plug_data } },
-    }
+  local res = {}
+
+  -- Only advertise the source-level action when the client explicitly asks
+  -- for `source` actions via `only`.

comment_data.body: As far as I remember, the *very* strict rule about LSP related features in Nvim is "it must be part of the official specification". Any other functionality is out of scope and even not welcome. I've seen this applied several times very strictly.

comment_data.body: Indeed, I've often been the one enforcing such mentality. However this isn't a deviation from the protocol, I'm just matching the behavior with what I believe most users are familiar with (or expect). I'm also open for us to do something different than VS Code if we desire.

comment_data.body:
> However this isn't a deviation from the protocol, I'm just matching the behavior with what I believe most users are familiar with (or expect).

I would still consider important to uniformly follow the established rules about LSP related functionality. If it is strict, it must be strict everywhere.

comment_regular_data.body:
The core idea behind code actions is to provide actions for a "plugin at cursor". For acting on all plugins there are `:write` and `:quit`.

This adds duplicating functionality via non trivial code complexity, for which I am really hesitant. Is there any particular reason for having this?

> The core idea behind code actions is to provide actions for a "plugin at cursor". 

I disagree. This is exactly why the `CodeActionKind` of `source` exists (which is what I'm using here). Examples of useful refactors like that are "Add all missing imports" or "Fix all lint errors", which other popular LSP servers provide.

> For acting on all plugins there are `:write` and `:quit`.

Those aren't as discoverable as code actions. I like having a consistent flow via LSP actions that allows me to update all plugins via the same mechanism.

> This adds duplicating functionality via non trivial code complexity, for which I am really hesitant.

I don't consider duplication to be a valid counterargument here. If so then why did we add `:packupdate` when you can use `:=vim.pack.update()`?

> > The core idea behind code actions is to provide actions for a "plugin at cursor".
> 
> I disagree. This is exactly why the `CodeActionKind` of `source` exists (which is what I'm using here). Examples of useful refactors like that are "Add all missing imports" or "Fix all lint errors", which other popular LSP servers provide.

It was not about code actions in general, but about code actions for `vim.pack` confirmation buffer. As the "global actions" are handled via `:write` and `:quit`.

> Those aren't as discoverable as code actions. I like having a consistent flow via LSP actions that allows me to update all plugins via the same mechanism.

As interacting with users shows, it is in reverse: code actions are not as discoverable as `:write` for the buffer that is named "confirm".

> I don't consider duplication to be a valid counterargument here. If so then why did we add `:packupdate` when you can use `:=vim.pack.update()`?

The one is intended for interactive use and the other - for programmatic (that was suggested for interactive use for too long). Here both `:write` and "update all plugins" code action are interactive. I.e. two ways of doing the same thing.

---

I am still hesitant about adding this.

> I am still hesitant about adding this.

Feel free to close this then. I won't add an unwelcome feature.

After sleeping on it, the one benefit I could find of adding this is that it might serve as a reference for implementing `Source` kind code actions for the in-process LSP server. Yet I still don't think that this justifies adding a duplicating functionality and code complexity.

So I'll indeed close this. Sorry for the inconvenience.
@echasnovski I appreciate the discussion nonetheless. What do you think about including a message like "Run `:write` to confirm all updates" underneath `# Update` here?

> @echasnovski I appreciate the discussion nonetheless. What do you think about including a message like "Run `:write` to confirm all updates" underneath `# Update` here? A similar approach was what I went with in 'mini.deps', but it was much more verbose: The problem with showing any amount of this kind of helper message is that it is only needed once, maybe twice. After that it becomes not useful and only occupies extra screen line(s). I personally don't really like it due to this short lived usefulness. Also: - The "`:write` to confirm all" is already mentioned twice in relevant places, including the first "Basic install and management" example that everybody new is expected to be familiar with. - If there is a note about `:write`, there probably should be a note about how to *not do* the update (i.e. "Run `:close` to skip all updates"). Which makes it two extra lines. The upside is that the actual implementation can probably be concise via one extra virtual line. Like this: ```diff diff --git a/runtime/ftplugin/nvim-pack.lua b/runtime/ftplugin/nvim-pack.lua index f4c5ba56ac..bdb2313c7e 100644 --- a/runtime/ftplugin/nvim-pack.lua +++ b/runtime/ftplugin/nvim-pack.lua @@ -14,6 +14,7 @@ end local header_hl_groups = { Error = 'DiagnosticError', Update = 'DiagnosticWarn', Same = 'DiagnosticHint' } local cur_header_hl_group = nil +local update_msg_lines = { { { 'Run `:write` to confirm all updates', 'Dimmed' } } } local lines = vim.api.nvim_buf_get_lines(0, 0, -1, false) for i, l in ipairs(lines) do @@ -24,6 +25,9 @@ for i, l in ipairs(lines) do -- Header 1 cur_header_hl_group = header_hl_groups[cur_group] hi_range(i, 0, l:len(), cur_header_hl_group) + if cur_group == 'Update' then + vim.api.nvim_buf_set_extmark(0, ns, i - 1, 0, { virt_lines = update_msg_lines }) + end elseif l:find('^## (.+)$') ~= nil then -- Header 2 with possibly "(not active)" suffix hi_range(i, 0, l:len(), cur_header_hl_group) ``` As this is more a UI taste thing which can be done relatively concisely, I won't have strong opinions on this. But would still vote against it.

> The problem with showing any amount of this kind of helper message is that it is only needed once, maybe twice. After that it becomes not useful and only occupies extra screen line(s).

This is true, but we do need some sort of pattern for showing ephemeral UI hints. Perhaps in a floatwin or virttext that floats near the top-right of the buffer. And we definitely don't want them to be verbose, and probably at some point we'll need a way to disable them... but it is something we need to think about

> This is true, but we do need some sort of pattern for showing ephemeral UI hints. Perhaps in a floatwin or virttext that floats near the top-right of the buffer. And we definitely don't want them to be verbose, and probably at some point we'll need a way to disable them... but it is something we need to think about

If it is an on demand "show me hints" type of thing, then I am all for it. The plugin ecosystem seems to have settled on using `g?` buffer local mapping to show hints about how to use special buffers. Not sure how much Rot13 encoding is useful to have this mapping more global.

If it can be disabled, ideally per "source", then I am also all for it.

The problem indeed is that there is no established pattern for either of this. So these helper lines become a burden real quick.
`g?` is a reasonable pattern until proven otherwise, so maybe we can do that for now. It could go to a targeted help section for vim.pack

> `g?` is a reasonable pattern until proven otherwise, so maybe we can do that for now. It could go to a targeted help section for vim.pack If it just opens a `:help` page, then I don't think it has much value over users typing `:help vim.pack` manually (which is what they probably are expected to know how to do). The point is that in plugins it provides concise overview of basic keymaps, maybe more. Here is how it looks in 'mini.files', for example (other plugins have something similar in either new floating window or the same floating window):

> * The "`:write` to confirm all" is already mentioned twice in relevant places, including the first "Basic install and management" example that everybody new is expected to be familiar with.
> * If there is a note about `:write`, there probably should be a note about how to _not do_ the update (i.e. "Run `:close` to skip all updates"). Which makes it two extra lines.

Even after reading the `vim.pack` documentation multiple times I still forget about these without having the visual hint. 2 extra lines doesn't seem outrageous and do provide a useful reminder of how to efficiently use the package manager.

@echasnovski since you implemented this (twice counting `mini.pack`) these hints might feel redundant and unnecessary, but for users whose memory quickly gets garbage collected I find it valuable :)

> Even after reading the `vim.pack` documentation multiple times I still forget about these without having the visual hint. 2 extra lines doesn't seem outrageous and do provide a useful reminder of how to efficiently use the package manager.

This seems to be more of a question about the assumptions for user's prior knowledge. Currently the basic assumption is that `:help vim.pack.update()` is read and understood before using it. If user reading and understanding help is not assumed, then this can evolve into "I am new and forget about what 'Run `:write`' means. Having it more explicit like 'Type `:`, `write`, `<Enter>`' would have helped me.".

Although I do agree that "`:write` to confirm" is (currently) a new-ish concept, there is no similar helper indication in Nvim specific special buffers that show information:

- The `:checkhealth` doesn't have a note about how to close the tabpage or delete the buffer. And it feels like the majority of users don't use tabpages that often, so they might forget how to do it.
- The `:InspectTree` doesn't have a note about what is happening when cursor moves or how to close the window.
- The `:EditQuery` *does have notes*, but it is not meant to show information spanning more than one screen.

I've thought about this type of questions more than once and don't think my opinion here will change:

- Having constantly shown information about how to use a special buffer becomes redundant and harmful really quickly.
- I am all for having a unified approach for on-demand showing helpful buffer-local information. But I won't be the one designing and advocating for it.
- If these opinions do not align with overall Nvim design, I of course won't block adding these. There is a [concise way of adding this](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39848#issuecomment-4495970755) without breaking too many tests. If there is a consensus that adding a note about `:quit` is also worth it, then probably adding the note always below the first line is better:

    ```diff
    diff --git a/runtime/ftplugin/nvim-pack.lua b/runtime/ftplugin/nvim-pack.lua
    index f4c5ba56ac..eb335e10c5 100644
    --- a/runtime/ftplugin/nvim-pack.lua
    +++ b/runtime/ftplugin/nvim-pack.lua
    @@ -15,6 +15,12 @@ local header_hl_groups =
       { Error = 'DiagnosticError', Update = 'DiagnosticWarn', Same = 'DiagnosticHint' }
     local cur_header_hl_group = nil
     
    +local update_msg_lines = {
    +  { { 'Run `:write` to confirm all updates', 'Dimmed' } },
    +  { { 'Run `:quit` to cancel all updates', 'Dimmed' } },
    +}
    +vim.api.nvim_buf_set_extmark(0, ns, 0, 0, { virt_lines = update_msg_lines })
    +
     local lines = vim.api.nvim_buf_get_lines(0, 0, -1, false)
     for i, l in ipairs(lines) do
       local cur_group = l:match('^# (%S+)')
    ```
 
> @echasnovski since you implemented this (twice counting `mini.pack`) these hints might feel redundant and unnecessary, but for users whose memory quickly gets garbage collected I find it valuable :)

This is not only my personal experience, but also a result of (at least several instances of) feedback. There was even one along the lines of "I didn't know what to do when this buffer opened since I didn't read documentation. But the name has `confirm` so I tried `:write` and it worked!".

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

title: feat(pack): code action for updating all plugins
